front: What is the core principle behind the gradient descent optimization algorithm?
back: Gradient descent is an iterative first-order optimization algorithm used to find a local minimum of a differentiable function. The core principle is to take steps proportional to the negative of the gradient (or approximate gradient) of the function at the current point, because this is the direction of steepest descent.

front: Write the update rule for the standard gradient descent algorithm.
back: The update rule is: x_{k+1} = x_k - α * ∇f(x_k), where x_k is the current parameter vector, α is the learning rate (step size), and ∇f(x_k) is the gradient of the objective function at x_k.

front: What is the critical role of the learning rate (α) in gradient descent?
back: The learning rate (α) controls the size of the steps taken towards the minimum. If it is too small, convergence will be very slow. If it is too large, the algorithm may overshoot the minimum, fail to converge, or even diverge.

front: What is a key assumption about the objective function for gradient descent to be applicable?
back: The objective function must be differentiable. Gradient descent relies on computing the gradient ∇f(x), so the function must have a well-defined first derivative at the points being evaluated.

front: What is a common convergence criterion used to stop the gradient descent iterations?
back: Common criteria include: 1) When the norm of the gradient ||∇f(x_k)|| becomes smaller than a predefined tolerance ε, indicating a near-stationary point. 2) When the change in the objective function value |f(x_{k+1}) - f(x_k)| is smaller than ε. 3) After a fixed maximum number of iterations.
